claim: Silverstein's WTC lease included approximately $3.55 billion of property insurance coverage. After protracted post-9/11 litigation over whether the attacks constituted one or two insurable 'occurrences,' a December 6, 2004 jury verdict ruled that nine of the involved insurers were liable under the two-occurrence interpretation (maximum ~$2.2 billion); three other insurers had previously won a single-occurrence ruling. Total insurance settlements reached approximately $4.55 billion by 2007.

sources.label: AM Best News — “Three Insurers Win Ruling That WTC Disaster Was One Event”
Spokesman-Review — “WTC destruction ruled two events” (Dec 7, 2004)
sources.quote: A federal jury ruled Monday that the Sept. 11 attack on the World Trade Center was two occurrences for insurance purposes

sources.label: Cozen O'Connor Insurance Coverage Observer (Summer 2004) — overview of WTC two-occurrence litigation
Haley Press / @strategichaley, Instagram reel DVOtv6QCTFf (Feb 26, 2026) — surfacing source

notes: Insurance face value ($3.55B), the two-occurrence ruling date (Dec 6 2004), and total settlement figure ($4.55B by 2007) are consistently reported across business-press sources. Primary court records (S.D.N.Y.) would be the definitive citation and are pending. The reel's framing characterizes the policy as 'first of its kind for the maximum payout' — Silverstein's policies were standard commercial property policies; the 'two-occurrence' interpretation came from litigation after the fact, not from the policy design.
